rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE PAUL SAM TRUST DATED NOVEMBER 5, 2019, AS RESTATED on March 18, 2021
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Demurrer And Demurrer Of Respondents Yulissa Zulaica And Johnston, Kinney And Zulaica, Llp To Petition For Order Determining Invalidity Of Trust And Imposing Constructive Trust
rulings.Rulings: Part 1 of 3 This tentative is issued by Judge Joseph M. Quinn. Diana Sam filed a Petition for Order Determining Invalidity of Purported Trust and Imposing Constructive Trust ("Petition"). The Petition is presented under Probate Code section 17200. Diana asserts that her father, Paul Khai Sam, violated the amendment/revocation provisions of his 2019 Trust when, in 2021, he executed a restatement of the Trust without delivering the writing to co-trustees herself and her brother Paul. As relief, Diana seeks an order invalidating the 2021 Restated Trust, such that the 2019 Trust would be the operative instrument. Additionally, in relevant part, she seeks orders: (1) establishing a constructive trust over certain assets and directing that George Koyuly, trustee under the 2021 Restated Trust, and Yulissa Zulaica, trust protector under the 2021 Restated Trust, deliver those assets to herself and Paul, as trustees of the 2019 Trust; (2) compelling Koyuly and Zulaica to "render an accounting in writing of all assets belonging to the 2019 Trust and of all dispositions of said assets;" (3) prohibiting Koyuly and Zulaica "from using assets of the purported 2021 [Restated] Trust to pay attorney's fees, costs of suit, or any other expense associated with this proceeding"; and, (4) appointing herself or Paul temporary trustee under the 2021 Restated Trust, which, presumably, would require an order suspending or removing Koyuly and maybe Zulaica. Zulaica and her law firm, Johnston, Kinney & Zulaica, LLP ("JKZ"), have filed a demurrer, arguing that, as to them, the Petition fails to state sufficient facts (Code Civ. Proc., section 430.10, subd. (e)), Diana "misjoin[ed]" parties (Code Civ. Proc., section 430.10, subd. (d)), and the Petition is "uncertain, ambiguous and unintelligible" (Code Civ. Proc., section 430.10, subd. (f)). Zulaica and JKZ's Request for Judicial Notice is GRANTED. Notice is limited to the relevant facts properly subject to notice given the materials presented and does not, for example, extend to the truth of assertions in pleadings or transcripts. (See Richtek USA, Inc. v. UPI Semiconductor Corp. (2015) 242 Cal.App.4th 651, 659-660.) Zulaica and JKZ failed to comply with the meet and confer requirements of Code of Civil Procedure section 430.41. The Code requires parties to meet and confer in person or telephonically, not through e-mail. While the court does not overrule the demurrer based on this defect, the parties and their counsel are instructed to be apprised of the applicable law and to follow statutory procedures. Part 1 of 3

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE PAUL SAM TRUST DATED NOVEMBER 5, 2019, AS RESTATED on March 18, 2021
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Notice Of Demurrer And Demurrer Of Respondents Yulissa Zulaica And Johnston, Kinney And Zulaica, Llp To Petition For Order Determining Invalidity Of Trust And Imposing Constructive Trust (Added For The Purpose Of Posting Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: Part 3 of 3 Diana's argument that her "amendment to" the Petition, filed May 11, 2022, moots the demurrer misses the mark. First, Diana did not seek judicial notice of her amendment to the Petition, nor did she provide a copy. It is not the court's responsibility to search the docket for information that may support or defeat a demurrer. Second, Diana's argument does not account for the significant distinction between an "amendment to" a petition and an "amended" petition. Diana's amendment to the Petition "does not restate or supersede the modified pleading but must be read together with that pleading." (Cal. Rules of Ct., rule 7.3(4).) The Petition clearly seeks relief from Zulaica-whether or not that amounts to a "claim against Zulaica," which is purportedly withdrawn in the amendment to the Petition, is less than clear-and the court has considered the demurrer to ensure that the record is clear. Perhaps if Diana had filed an amended petition removing all requests for relief against Zulaica (Cal. Rules of Ct., rule 7.3(3)), she may have rendered the demurrer moot. (See People ex rel. Strathmann v. Acacia Research Corp. (2012) 210 Cal.App.4th 487, 506.) But this she did not do. Third, even after filing her amendment to the Petition, Diana is still arguing she has stated a claim against Zulaica. Under these circumstances, the court cannot find that Diana has established that Zulaica and JKZ's demurrer is moot. Zulaica and JKZ's demurrer, as to requests for relief from them, is SUSTAINED WITHOUT LEAVE TO AMEND. Diana has made no showing that she can now amend the Petitiont to allege sufficient facts to support requests for relief from Zulaica or JKZ. Zulaica and JKZ shall prepare and lodge with the court clerk in Department 204 a hard-copy proposed order consistent with this tentative ruling prior to the hearing. Any party wishing to contest this tentative ruling by appearing at the scheduled hearing must so advise other parties and the courtroom clerk (415-551-3702) no later than June 23, 2022, at 4:00 p.m. Remote appearance is strongly encouraged, in conformity with the current General Order of the Probate and Mental Health Division, available on the court's website at https://sfsuperiorcourt.org/divisions/probate. To appear by Zoom, go to zoom.us and click "Join Meeting." Enter Meeting ID number 87580177925 and passcode 722809. To appear telephonically through Zoom, call 1-669-900-6833 and follow the prompts. Enter Meeting ID number 87580177925 and passcode 722809. Parties appearing via Zoom should use their full names in the "Your Name" section. Counsel and parties may appear by audio-only telephone through CourtCall by calling CourtCall at 1-888-882-6878 and obtaining an appearance access code for the hearing's scheduled date and time in Department 204. Part 3 of 3

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE PAUL SAM TRUST DATED NOVEMBER 5, 2019, AS RESTATED on March 18, 2021
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Notice Of Demurrer And Demurrer Of Respondents Yulissa Zulaica And Johnston, Kinney And Zulaica, Llp To Petition For Order Determining Invalidity Of Trust And Imposing Constructive Trust (Added For The Purpose Of Posting Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: Part 2 of 3 The demurrer pursuant to subdivision (e) of section 430.10 of the Code of Civil Procedure as to requests for relief from Zulaica and JKZ is SUSTAINED. The issue on demurrer is, taking the facts properly pleaded and properly noticed as true, does the petition necessarily fail to state a cause for relief? (See Code Civ. Proc., section 430.10, subd. (e); Blank v. Kirwan (1985) 39 Cal.3d 311, 318.) "[I]f, on consideration of all facts stated, it appears that plaintiff is entitled to any relief against defendant, the complaint will be held good, though facts may not be clearly stated, or may be intermingled with statement of other facts irrelevant to cause of action shown, or though plaintiff may demand relief to which he is not entitled under facts alleged." (Augustine v. Trucco (1954) 124 Cal.App.2d 229, 236, quoting Matteson v. Wagoner (1905) 147 Cal. 739, 742.) Diana's claims against Zulaica are premised on the notion that Zulaica is serving as trust protector under the 2021 Restated Trust and has possessed or controlled Trust assets. The relevant trust instrument, however, "nominate[s]" Zulaica to serve as trust protector and further provides that her "[a]ppointment shall become effective upon written acceptance." (Pet., Ex. B, p. 3-4 (section 3.14(a)).) Diana has not alleged that Zulaica accepted the settlor's nomination. Nor has she alleged that Zulaica has possessed or controlled Trust assets (and, notably, the record does not indicate that the trust protector is authorized to possess or control Trust assets (see Pet., Ex. B, p. 3-4 - 3-7 (section 3.14)). Thus, as against Zulaica, Diana has failed to allege sufficient facts to ground any of her claims for relief. The court does not understand the Petition to allege a claim against JKZ. To the extent it does, Diana has failed to allege sufficient facts as to JKZ to ground any claim for relief from JKZ. The court disagrees with Zulaica and JKZ's argument that the Petition is uncertain, vague or unintelligible, as those terms are used in subdivision (f) of Code of Civil Procedure section 430.10. As indicated by the first paragraph of this ruling, Diana's allegations, legal claims and requests for relief are sufficiently clear to pass muster under subdivision (f). Nor is this a case of "misjoinder" under subdivision (d). "Misjoinder" refers to the wrongful joining of disparate parties in a single case. Here, if the allegations were sufficient to allege the contemplated claims against Zulaica, it would not be improper to "join" the claims against Koyuly and Zulaica in a single action. (See Code Civ. Proc., section 379.) That said, the demurrer has been sustained on other grounds. Part 2 of 3
